name: First Aid and Wound Care
tagline: Stopping bleeding, cleaning wounds, splinting injuries, and preventing infection when hospitals and pharmacies are gone
problem:
Most people who die after battles, factory accidents, and kitchen slips do not die from the wound in the first minute — they die from blood loss hours later, from infection days later, or from fever because someone packed dirt into a laceration. Modern emergency departments hide this truth behind ambulances and antibiotics; remove the infrastructure and the same bodies obey the same physiology.

The first aid problem is triage under uncertainty: you cannot image every fracture, culture every wound, or wait for a sterile field. You must decide which injuries demand immediate motionless packaging, which can walk, and which will declare themselves later with swelling lines up the limb. Wrong triage wastes the one person who could fetch water; right triage is a moral skill learned by drill.

The wound-care problem is contamination control without sterile industry: boiled cloth is not operating-theatre sterile but beats unwashed shirt; honey and sugar have osmotic effects on some wounds but are not universal magic; pressure dressings stop venous ooze but do not fix arterial jets. Teaching people what they cannot fix — open abdomens, skull depressions with changing consciousness, partial amputations with pale stumps — is as important as teaching pressure bandages, because false confidence kills.

Contraindications: do not apply tourniquets for minor bleeding — ischaemia damages limbs; do not pour alcohol into deep cavities; do not reduce fractures blindly if sensation is lost after manipulation. Overdose of pain relief is less common without drugs, but herbal toxicity is real. Sterile conditions for surgery are not reproduced by campfire ash — professional verification is required before use on a human being for any intervention beyond basic first aid and wound cleaning in non-sterile environments.
overview:
Bleeding control begins with direct pressure: gloved or clean-clothed hand on wound, add bulk dressing, elevate if not contraindicated. Arterial bright spurting may need a tourniquet wide and proximal — note time written on forehead tape culture. Release only in systems that can surgically salvage — in collapse, tourniquet means limb life versus life life; train that horror honestly.

Wound cleaning means remove visible foreign material, irrigate with copious clean water boiled and cooled if possible, and dress without reintroducing fingers. Debride hanging dead tissue only if trained — otherwise cover and evacuate.

Burns: stop burning process with cool clean water not ice; cover with loose sterile or clean dressings; fluid losses scale with surface area — small children die faster.

Splints immobilise along joints above and below fracture; check circulation after every wrap pass — blue fingers mean loosen now. Slings support collarbone and wrist patterns.

Fever after wound: infection sign — open dressing if safe, photograph smell colour if literate, increase fluids if drinking possible, and isolate patient to reduce epidemic spread in camp.

This entry is not a substitute for professional medical care when it exists.

principles.name: ABC mindset
principles.explanation: Airway, breathing, circulation — unconscious patient may need positioning before wound play.

principles.name: Pressure before tourniquet
principles.explanation: Most external bleeding stops with well-applied pressure and packing.

principles.name: Warmth and glucose
principles.explanation: Shocky patients lose temperature; hypothermia worsens coagulopathy.

principles.name: Infection sentinels
principles.explanation: Rising pain after initial relief, spreading redness, red streaks, fever — escalate isolation and care plans.

components.name: Pressure dressings
components.madeFrom: Sterile gauze if stockpiled; boiled cloth if not

components.name: Splints and padding
components.madeFrom: Boards, rolled magazines, padded aluminium strips

components.name: Irrigation syringe or clean bottle
components.criticalNote: Pressure irrigation drives bacteria deeper if uncontrolled — training matters

components.name: Casualty card
components.function: Tracks time of injury, interventions, vitals

rawMaterials.name: Clean water
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Recovered potable loop water per habitat protocol only
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Boil for irrigation if biological contamination possible

rawMaterials.name: Cloth
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Sterile gauze from medical cargo until laundry sterilisers exist
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Cut squares; store in metal tin baked dry

rawMaterials.name: Structural splint stock
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Carbon fibre strips from equipment repair kits

buildSteps.title: Scene safety and gloves if any exist
buildSteps.description: Remove patient from road, fire, or collapse risk. Don gloves from kit or use clean plastic bags as last resort.
buildSteps.warningNote: Moving spinal injury without stabilisation can paralyse — hold head inline if mechanism fits

buildSteps.title: Apply direct pressure until bleeding changes character
buildSteps.description: Hand on wound; add folded cloth; maintain uninterrupted minutes.

buildSteps.title: If arterial and uncontrolled, apply wide tourniquet and mark time
buildSteps.description: Place proximal; tighten until bleeding stops; never narrow cord — nerve injury.
buildSteps.warningNote: Tourniquet pain is severe — psychological support; limb loss risk is real

buildSteps.title: Irrigate visibly dirty wounds after bleeding controlled
buildSteps.description: Stream from bottle with puncture cap; remove gravel with forceps if trained.
buildSteps.warningNote: Do not irrigate embedded objects that tamponade vessels — stabilise and transport

buildSteps.title: Splint, sling, and reassess circulation every fifteen minutes
buildSteps.description: Check colour, warmth, cap refill distal to injury; loosen wraps if compromised.
buildSteps.warningNote: Scissors near skin in trembling hands — cut along long axis away from body

history.event: Battlefield surgery codifies wound packing and arrow extraction.
ATLS and civilian first aid programmes standardise triage.

impact:
First aid is the technology of buying time until better care exists — or of accepting limits honestly when it never will.

Every settlement should rehearse it quarterly.

warnings: Contraindications: suspected spinal injury with neuro deficit — immobilise spine before movement; crush injury compartments — do not apply tight circumferential wraps without monitoring.
Overdose risk: minimal for non-drug interventions; if using any stocked analgesic, follow printed dosing — never double-dose children by adult scales.
Sterility: field dressings are cleaner than dirt, not sterile — deep penetrating wounds need professional care when available.
Professional verification is required before use on a human being. This is especially important for any procedure beyond basic first aid, public-health triage, and non-invasive wound cleaning.

sources.title: WHO — Injuries
